questions.question: How many ribs must be visible above the diaphragm to define hyperinflation in a neonatal chest X-ray?
questions.detail: Neonatal hyperinflation is determined by counting more than 6 anterior or 8 posterior ribs at the level of the diaphragm.

questions.question: Which of the following is a classic sign of hyperinflation on a babygram?
choices: More vertical course of the ribs
questions.detail: Helpful signs of hyperinflation include flattening of the diaphragms and ribs taking on a more horizontal appearance.

questions.question: Which neonatal lung conditions are associated with HIGH lung volumes and perihilar streaking (the MNoP mnemonic)?
questions.detail: Meconium, Aspiration, and Non-GBS Neonatal Pneumonia typically present with high lung volumes and a streaky pattern.

questions.question: Meconium Aspiration is most commonly seen in which patient population?
questions.detail: Meconium aspiration is more common in term or post-mature babies, often associated with stress/hypoxia.

questions.question: What is the classic radiographic buzzword for Meconium Aspiration?
questions.detail: The hallmark of Meconium Aspiration is a "ropy appearance" with hyperinflation and alternating areas of atelectasis.

questions.question: Why does Meconium Aspiration cause hyperinflation despite being "sticky poop" in the lungs?
choices: It causes chemical burns that expand alveoli
It acts like a miniature ball-valve, causing air trapping
It is always associated with a PDA
It is purely due to the baby crying harder
questions.detail: The meconium acts as a ball-valve, allowing air in but not out, leading to air trapping and increased volumes.

questions.question: Pneumothorax occurs in what percentage of Meconium Aspiration cases?
questions.detail: Pneumothorax is a common complication of meconium aspiration, occurring in 20-40% of cases.

questions.question: What is the classic clinical history associated with Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn (TTN)?
questions.detail: The classic history for TTN is a C-section (where the vagina doesn't squeeze the fluid out) or maternal sedation.

questions.question: When do radiographic findings of Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn (TTN) typically resolve?
choices: They progress to chronic lung disease
questions.detail: Findings peak at one day and are typically resolved by 3 days.

questions.question: Radiographic coarse interstitial markings and fluid in the fissures on a term infant's CXR suggest:
questions.detail: Coarse markings and fluid in the fissures are classic radiographic features of TTN.

questions.question: Surfactant-Deficient Disease (SDD/RDS) is primarily a disease of:
questions.detail: SDD is a disease of pre-mature kids who lack surfactant to keep alveoli open.

questions.question: Which radiographic findings characterize Surfactant-Deficient Disease (SDD)?
choices: High lung volumes and ropy opacities
Low lung volumes and bilateral granular opacities
High lung volumes and fluid in the fissures
Normal lung volumes and pleural effusions
questions.detail: SDD features low lung volumes and granular (sand-like) opacities.

questions.question: Which finding helps distinguish Group B Strep (GBS) Pneumonia from SDD on a babygram?
choices: SDD only occurs in term infants
questions.detail: Unlike SDD, Group B Strep Pneumonia often (25%) has pleural effusions.

questions.question: A normal chest X-ray at what time interval effectively excludes SDD?
questions.detail: A normal plain film at 6 hours effectively excludes SDD.

questions.question: What are the known risks of Surfactant Replacement Therapy?
questions.detail: Surfactant therapy increases the risk of pulmonary hemorrhage and Patent Ductus Arteriosus (PDA).

questions.question: Which type of neonatal pneumonia is the MOST common?
questions.detail: Group B Strep is the most common type of pneumonia in newborns.

questions.question: Pulmonary Interstitial Emphysema (PIE) is characteristically a consequence of:
questions.detail: PIE is a consequence of positive pressure ventilation "pulverizing" the lungs, causing air to escape into the interstitium.

questions.question: What is the characteristic radiographic appearance of Pulmonary Interstitial Emphysema (PIE)?
questions.detail: The radiographic buzzword for PIE is "linear lucencies" representing air in the interstitium.

questions.question: What is the recommended treatment for unilateral or localized PIE?
choices: Place the patient with the "bad side" down
questions.detail: The treatment for PIE is to switch ventilation methods or place the affected (bad) side down.

questions.question: Bronchopulmonary Dysplasia (BPD) / Chronic Lung Disease is defined by cystic lucencies and fibrosis after what postnatal age?
questions.detail: While PIE occurs in the first week, CLD/BPD is diagnosed after 3–4 weeks of life.

questions.question: What is the classic radiographic appearance of BPD?
choices: Alternating regions of fibrosis and hyper-aeration
Small heart and low lung volumes
questions.detail: The classic look of BPD is alternating fibrosis (coarse lines) and hyper-aeration (cysts) with "band-like opacities."

questions.question: "Pulmonary Hypoplasia" or "Tiny Lung" is characteristically associated with which sequence?
questions.detail: Potter Sequence (No kidneys → No pee → No amniotic fluid → Hypoplastic lungs) is the classic association for tiny lungs.

questions.question: Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernias (CDHs) are most commonly of which type?
questions.detail: Most CDHs are the Bochdalek type ("B is in the Back").

questions.question: On which side do most Bochdalek hernias occur?
questions.detail: Bochdalek hernias are typically posterior and occur on the left side.

questions.question: Which abdominal finding is virtually always present in a patient with a CDH?
questions.detail: Essentially all patients with a Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia have malrotation.

questions.question: Bronchopulmonary Sequestration is anatomically defined as "extra lung" that lacks:
choices: Communication with the airway and pulmonary arteries
questions.detail: Sequestrations have no communication with the normal airway or pulmonary arteries; they usually have an aortic feeder.

questions.question: Which type of sequestration is more common (75%) and typically presents in adolescence with recurrent infections?
questions.detail: Intralobar is more common and presents later in life with recurrent pneumonia in the same area.

questions.question: Which type of sequestration presents in infancy, often with associated congenital anomalies, and has its own pleural cover?
questions.detail: Extralobar presents in infancy and is associated with other "extra" findings like heart defects and CDH.

questions.question: What is the most common location for both Intralobar and Extralobar Sequestrations?
questions.detail: Both types of sequestration favor the Left Lower Lobe.

questions.question: Congenital Lobar Emphysema (CLE) most commonly involves which lobe?
questions.detail: CLE characteristically prefers the Left Upper Lobe.

questions.question: What is the initial radiographic appearance of CLE in a newborn?
questions.detail: In the first few days, the affected lobe is opaque as it clears fluid slowly; it then becomes progressively hyper-lucent.

questions.question: What is the definitive treatment for Congenital Lobar Emphysema (CLE)?
questions.detail: The treatment for CLE is surgical resection (lobectomy).

questions.question: Congenital Cystic Adenomatoid Malformation (CCAM/CPAM) is characterized by:
choices: Adenomatoid tissue replacing normal lung, usually in one lobe
Preference for the left upper lobe
questions.detail: CCAM is a malformation of adenomatoid tissue that replaces normal lung, typically localized to one lobe.

questions.question: How can you distinguish CCAM from Sequestration on imaging?
choices: CCAM is always on the left
Sequestration has a systemic arterial feeder (aorta)
There is no way to tell them apart
questions.detail: If you see a systemic arterial feeder, it is a Sequestration, not a CCAM.

questions.question: What is the MOST common primary lung malignancy in children?
questions.detail: PPB is the most common primary lung malignancy in children.

questions.question: A Pleuropulmonary Blastoma (PPB) in a young child has a 10% association with which renal condition?
questions.detail: PPB is associated with multilocular cystic nephroma in about 10% of cases.

questions.question: What is the MOST common primary benign lung mass in children?
questions.detail: IMT is the most common primary benign lung mass in children.

questions.question: Where is the ideal position for the tip of an Umbilical Venous Catheter (UVC)?
questions.detail: The ideal spot for a UVC tip is at the IVC-RA junction.

questions.question: Where should an Umbilical Artery Catheter (UAC) be placed to avoid the renal arteries?
questions.detail: Placement should be high (T8-T10) or low (L3-L5) to avoid the origins of the renal arteries.

questions.question: Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) in a child has a classic predilection for causing atelectasis in which lobe?
questions.detail: RSV often causes segmental or lobar atelectasis, with a classic predilection for the RUL.

questions.question: A solitary "round pneumonia" in a child younger than 8 is most commonly caused by:
questions.detail: S. Pneumonia is the typical culprit for round pneumonia in the pediatric population.

questions.question: What is the take-home message for a "round pneumonia" seen on a pediatric CXR?
choices: Perform emergent CT to exclude cancer
Follow up with X-ray to confirm resolution
questions.detail: A round pneumonia does not need a CT; follow-up with X-ray to confirm resolution is the next step.

questions.question: Swyer-James syndrome is the classic "unilateral lucent lung" that occurs after:
choices: A viral lung infection in childhood (post-infectious obliterative bronchiolitis)
Congenital absence of the pulmonary artery
questions.detail: It results from post-infectious obliterative bronchiolitis following a viral infection.

questions.question: In Swyer-James syndrome, the affected lucent lobe is typically:
questions.detail: In Swyer-James, the size of the affected lucent lobe is smaller than normal.

questions.question: What is the leading cause of death in pediatric Sickle Cell patients?
questions.detail: Acute Chest Syndrome is the leading cause of death in these patients.

questions.question: Which radiographic clue helps identify a patient as having Sickle Cell disease?
questions.detail: Radiographic clues include a big heart, bone infarcts (humeral heads), and H-shaped vertebrae.

questions.question: Cystic Fibrosis (CF) characteristically causes bronchiectasis in which lung distribution?
choices: Only in the Right Middle Lobe
questions.detail: Cystic Fibrosis features bronchiectasis with an upper lobe (apical) predominance.

questions.question: Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia (PCD) is distinguished from CF by which feature?
questions.detail: PCD is characterized by lower lobe predominant bronchiectasis, whereas CF is upper lobe.

questions.question: What percentage of Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia (PCD) patients have Kartagener's Syndrome (situs inversus)?
questions.detail: Only approximately 50% of patients with PCD have Kartagener's Syndrome.

questions.question: The "Scimitar Sword" sign is pathognomonic for:
questions.detail: The Scimitar Sword sign represents an anomalous pulmonary vein draining a hypoplastic lung.

questions.question: What is the most common anterior mediastinal "mass" in children under age 5?
questions.detail: A normal thymus is the most common anterior mediastinal "mass" in young children.

questions.question: The "Sail Sign" in a pediatric chest X-ray represents the:
questions.detail: The normal triangular thymus creates the "sail sign" on a CXR.

questions.question: "Thymic Rebound" occurs after acute stress (chemo, radiation) and may be tricky because it is:
questions.detail: During the recovery phase, the rebounding thymus can be PET avid, which can be confused with disease recurrence.

questions.question: What is the most common abnormal mediastinal mass in children over age 10?
questions.detail: In children over 10, an anterior mediastinal mass is most likely lymphoma.

questions.question: Which type of lymphoma is far more common in children and involves the thymus 90% of the time?
questions.detail: Hodgkin Lymphoma is much more common in the pediatric population.

questions.question: Calcification in an anterior mediastinal mass of a child that has not yet been treated suggests:
questions.detail: Lymphoma typically does not calcify unless it has been treated; pre-treatment calcification favors Teratoma.

questions.question: Anterior mediastinal Germ Cell Tumors (GCTs) are strongly associated with which syndrome?
questions.detail: Mediastinal Germ Cell Tumors are characteristically linked to Klinefelter syndrome.

questions.question: Which GCT flavor is described as bulky, lobulated, and characteristically "straddles the midline"?
questions.detail: Seminomas characteristically straddle the midline in the anterior mediastinum.

questions.question: Middle mediastinal lymphadenopathy in an adolescent with hilar adenopathy, splenomegaly, and fatigue is likely:
questions.detail: This classic clinical triad is highly suggestive of Mononucleosis.

questions.question: Which middle mediastinal duplication cyst is characteristically close to the esophagus and may have air-fluid levels?
questions.detail: Enteric cysts abut the esophagus and can communicate with its lumen, potentially showing air-fluid levels.

questions.question: What is the most common posterior mediastinal mass in a child under age 10?
questions.detail: Neuroblastoma is the most common posterior mass in children under 10.

questions.question: On a CXR, which finding associated with a posterior mediastinal mass suggests Neuroblastoma?
questions.detail: Neuroblastoma often involves the adjacent ribs, causing splaying and erosions.

questions.question: Along the neuroblastic spectrum, which tumor is the most mature and found in older children (2nd decade)?
questions.detail: Ganglioneuroma is the most mature and benign form in the neuroblastic spectrum.

questions.question: A "Neuroenteric Cyst" in the posterior mediastinum is classically associated with:
questions.detail: Neuroenteric cysts are characteristically linked to vertebral anomalies like scoliosis or hemivertebrae.

questions.question: Extramedullary Hematopoiesis appears as smooth paraspinal masses typically found:
questions.detail: These masses are smooth and typically located in the lower chest, below T6.

questions.question: Which radiographic clue should make you think of Extramedullary Hematopoiesis?
questions.detail: Look for coarse bone trabeculation and potentially a clinical history of anemia.

questions.question: If you see a "Big Fucking Mass" (BFM) in the chest of a child AGE 10+, you should think:
questions.detail: In children over 10, a BFM is likely an Askin Tumor (PNET/Ewings); look for bone (rib) destruction.

questions.question: If you see a "Big Fucking Mass" (BFM) in the chest of a child AGE < 2, you should think:
questions.detail: In toddlers under 2, a BFM in the chest is likely a Pleuropulmonary Blastoma (PPB).

questions.question: The primary discriminator for pediatric posterior mediastinal masses is:
questions.detail: Age is the primary discriminator: under 10 favors malignancy (Neuroblastoma); 2nd decade favors benignity (Ganglioneuroma).

questions.question: "Cervicothoracic Sign": If a mediastinal mass goes above the level of the clavicles on a frontal CXR, it is located in the:
questions.detail: If the mass borders are visible above the clavicles, it must be in the posterior mediastinum.

questions.question: The "LeCompte Maneuver" is the surgical signature for a corrected:
questions.detail: The LeCompte maneuver results in the pulmonary artery draped over the aorta in a corrected D-transposition.

questions.question: What is the most common congenital heart disease (overall population)?
questions.detail: Bicuspid Aortic Valve (2%) is likely the most common; VSD is also very common.

questions.question: "Supra-valvular Aortic Stenosis" is the buzzword for which syndrome?
questions.detail: Williams Syndrome is characteristically associated with supra-valvular aortic stenosis.

questions.question: "Bicuspid Aortic Valve and Coarctation" is the classic cardiac buzzword for:
questions.detail: Turner Syndrome patients have a high incidence of bicuspid valves and aortic coarctation.

questions.question: What is the infectious etiology of Rheumatic heart disease?
questions.detail: Rheumatic heart disease is an immune-modulated response to Group A Strep infection.

questions.question: A massive "Box Shaped" heart on a newborn CXR is the Aunt Minnie for:
questions.detail: Ebstein's anomaly features a massive "box-shaped" heart due to a hypoplastic tricuspid valve.

questions.question: Ebstein Anomaly is classically associated with maternal use of:
questions.detail: Lithium use is the classic (though rare) association for Ebstein anomaly.

questions.question: Tricuspid Atresia is characteristically associated with which abdominal finding?
questions.detail: There is a recognized association between Tricuspid Atresia and Asplenia.

questions.question: Which cyanotic heart disease is the most common cause of cyanosis in the first 24 hours of life?
questions.detail: D-Transposition is the leading cause of early cyanosis (first 24 hours).

questions.question: D-Transposition of the Great Arteries is most common in infants of:
questions.detail: Transposition is seen most commonly in infants of diabetic mothers.

questions.question: An "Egg on a String" radiographic heart appearance is characteristic of:
questions.detail: D-Transposition classically appears as an "egg on a string" on a CXR.

questions.question: A "Boot Shaped" heart (Coeur en Sabot) is characteristic of:
questions.detail: TOF classically presents as a "boot-shaped" heart.

questions.question: What is the most common cyanotic heart disease overall?
questions.detail: TOF is the most common overall cyanotic disease.

questions.question: What are the 4 classic findings of Tetralogy of Fallot?
choices: VSD, RVOT Obstruction, Overriding Aorta, RV Hypertrophy
ASD, LVOT Obstruction, PDA, LV Hypertrophy
VSD, Tricuspid Atresia, Coarctation, Right Arch
ASD, Pulmonary Stenosis, Small Heart, Boot shape
VSD, Aortic Stenosis, Interrupted Arch, LVH
questions.detail: The TOF tetrad includes VSD, RVOT obstruction, overriding aorta, and RV hypertrophy.

questions.question: What is the most common complication following surgery for TOF?
questions.detail: Pulmonary regurgitation is the most common post-operative issue for TOF.

questions.question: Truncus Arteriosus is strongly associated with which genetic syndrome?
questions.detail: Truncus and DiGeorge Syndrome are closely linked.

questions.question: The "Figure 3 Sign" on a CXR is the Aunt Minnie for:
questions.detail: The Figure 3 sign is classic for aortic coarctation.

questions.question: Rib notching in coarctation characteristically spares which ribs?
choices: All ribs on the right side
questions.detail: It spares the 1st and 2nd ribs because they are fed by the costocervical trunk.

questions.question: "Pseudocoarctation" is distinguished from true coarctation by the ABSENCE of:
choices: A pressure gradient, collateral formation, or rib notching
questions.detail: Pseudocoarctation has no pressure gradient, collateral formation, or rib notching.

questions.question: What is the most common fetal cardiac tumor, and what is its syndrome association?
questions.detail: Rhabdomyomas are the most common fetal cardiac tumors and are associated with TS.

questions.question: Which pediatric cardiac tumor typically involves the interventricular (IV) septum and is dark on T1 and T2?
questions.detail: Fibromas prefer the IV septum and are dark on both T1 and T2.

questions.question: On CXR, the "Oreo Cookie Sign" (two lucent lines surrounding a central opaque line) represents:
questions.detail: This sign describes pericardial fluid trapped between epicardial and pericardial fat layers.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of pericardial effusion in the general pediatric setting?
questions.detail: Uremia from renal failure is probably the most common cause.

questions.question: Which heart lesion is the most common indication for Pulmonary Artery Banding?
questions.detail: The single ventricle is the most common lesion requiring banding to reduce PA pressure.

questions.question: The "Ross Procedure" involves replacing a diseased aortic valve with the patient's own:
questions.detail: In the Ross Procedure, the pulmonary valve is moved to replace the aortic valve.

questions.question: A "Snowman" appearance on a pediatric CXR is characteristic of which type of Total Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Return (TAPVR)?
questions.detail: Type 1 (Supracardiac) drains above the heart, creating the snowman look.

questions.question: Which type of TAPVR causes a "full-on pulmonary edema look" in a newborn?
questions.detail: Type 3 drains below the diaphragm and characteristically becomes obstructed, causing pulmonary edema.

questions.question: "Asplenia" (Right Isomerism) is characteristically associated with which of the following?
choices: One fissure in the right lung
questions.detail: Asplenia is associated with a reversed Aorta/IVC relationship and two right sides.

questions.question: "Polysplenia" (Left Isomerism) is characteristically associated with:
choices: Two fissures in the right lung
questions.detail: Polysplenia is associated with azygos continuation (interrupted IVC).

questions.question: For multiple choice gamesmanship, a mediastinal mass with "Fat" density is most likely a:
questions.detail: Fat in a mediastinal mass indicates a Germ Cell Tumor (like a Teratoma).
